headline: Sedition Act Used 148 Times Since 2012 — 72% Against Opposition and Minorities
context: Lawyers for Liberty documented 148 Sedition Act charges between 2012 and 2025, with 72% targeting opposition politicians, journalists, and minority community leaders.

cards.big: The Sedition Act was enacted in 1948 by the British colonial government.
cards.sub: 148 charges since 2012. 72% targeted people who criticised government policy or defended minority rights.

cards.big: Section 3(1)(f) makes it seditious to question Malay special rights or Malay Rulers' sovereignty.
cards.sub: This effectively criminalises debate on constitutional provisions that affect all Malaysians.

cards.big: Pakatan Harapan promised to repeal the Sedition Act in 2018.
cards.sub: The promise was abandoned. The act remains in force under the same coalition in a different configuration.

cards.big: South Korea, Taiwan, and India all repealed equivalent sedition laws.
cards.sub: Malaysia retains a colonial instrument that even the colonial power (UK) repealed in 2009.

cards.big: A law written by colonial occupiers to suppress independence movements, now used to suppress democratic debate. The target changed. The tool did not.

The Sedition Act is incompatible with democratic governance. Its retention signals that political power considers certain questions too dangerous to ask. In a democracy, no question should be criminal.
